mnemonics.title: File Stream Types
mnemonics.content: FIFO: File Input (ifstream), File Output (ofstream), File Both (fstream). Think 'I Owe Frank' - I=Input, O=Output, F=Fstream (both)

mnemonics.title: File Modes
mnemonics.content: TRUNC destroys old file, APPend adds to end, Binary bytes only, ATE starts at end, IN reads, OUT writes. Remember: 'Take A Readable Book And Open Normally' - Trunc, App, Binary, Ate, In, Out, ios::noreplace (not in C++)

acronyms.usage: Used to read data from files, default mode ios::in
Used to write data to files, default mode ios::out | ios::trunc
Provides both input and output operations on files
Base class for stream objects, contains file mode flags
Positions the get (read) pointer in file
Positions the put (write) pointer in file
Returns current position of get pointer
Returns current position of put pointer

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a cursor blinking at a position in a file. Reading starts from cursor and moves forward. Writing overwrites at cursor or appends if at end.
Think of a file as a notebook: ios::in = reading page, ios::out = writing new page (erases old), ios::app = adding notes at end, ios::trunc = ripping out all pages to start fresh.
Arrows: ifstream has arrow pointing INTO program (reading), ofstream has arrow pointing OUT from program (writing), fstream has two-way arrows.
g = 'get' (reading), p = 'put' (writing). Both functions move a bookmark to different positions - beginning (0), current (cur), or end (end).

analogies.analogy: Think of file streams like different types of doors: ifstream is a door you can only walk through INTO a room (reading), ofstream is a door you can only walk OUT of a room (writing), fstream is a door you can use both ways.

File modes are like different ways to open a document: Read mode is like viewing a read-only PDF, Write mode is like opening a new Word document (erases old), Append mode is like opening a diary where you add new entries at the back.
Using getline() is like reading a book page by page - each call gets one complete line. Using >> is like reading word by word from a dictionary - each call gets one token separated by spaces.

Closing a file is like properly putting a book back on the shelf - it releases resources and ensures data is saved. If you don't close, the 'book' stays open (resources tied up) until the program ends.
